md: ### The center of mass of a triangle
A **median** of a triangle is a segment from a vertex to the **midpoint of the opposite side**. Every triangle has exactly three medians.

The three medians meet at a single point called the **centroid** $G$. The centroid is the **center of mass** (or **balance point**) of a triangle made of uniform material — if you cut a triangle out of stiff paper, it would balance perfectly on a pin placed at $G$.
Toggle on 'Centroid (medians)' below and watch the three dashed green lines from each vertex to the opposite midpoint.

blocks.title: The coordinate formula — disarmingly clean
blocks.body:
If the triangle has vertices at coordinates $A$, $B$, $C$, then the centroid is the **average** of the three:

$G = \dfrac{A + B + C}{3} = \left(\dfrac{x_A + x_B + x_C}{3},\; \dfrac{y_A + y_B + y_C}{3}\right).$

No weighted average, no fuss — just the plain average. This is why $G$ is the center of mass: it's the average of the vertex positions.

md: ### The 2:1 ratio
Here is the most famous fact about the centroid:
**The centroid divides each median in ratio 2:1, with the longer piece next to the vertex.**
If $M$ is the midpoint of the side opposite $A$, and $G$ is the centroid, then
So $G$ sits two-thirds of the way along each median from the vertex (or one-third of the way from the midpoint).

blocks.title: Why 2:1? (Sketch)
blocks.body:
Consider two medians from $A$ and $B$. They meet at some point $G$. Use vectors with origin at $A$ for clarity:
- Midpoint $M_a$ of $BC$ has position $(B + C)/2$ in $A$-centered coordinates.
- The median from $A$ is the segment from $0$ to $(B+C)/2$.
- The median from $B$ is from $B$ to the midpoint of $AC$, which is $C/2$.

Parametrize and solve for the intersection — you find $G = (B + C)/3$ in $A$-coords. So $AG = |(B+C)/3|$ and $GM_a = |(B+C)/2 - (B+C)/3| = |(B+C)/6|$. Ratio $AG : GM_a = 1/3 : 1/6 = 2 : 1$. ∎

The same argument works at every vertex.

md: ### Worked example
Triangle with vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(8, 3)$, $C(2, 8)$.
**Centroid:**
**Verify the 2:1 ratio with the median from $A$.**
Median from $A$ runs from $A(2,1)$ to $M_a(5, 5.5)$. Compute $AG$: distance from $A$ to $G$ = $\sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}$. Compute $GM_a$: distance from $G$ to $M_a$ = $\sqrt{1 + 2.25} = \sqrt{3.25}$.
Ratio: $\sqrt{13} / \sqrt{3.25} = \sqrt{4} = 2$. So $AG = 2 \cdot GM_a$, confirming $2:1$. ✓

blocks.title: Physical interpretation
blocks.body:
Pretend each vertex carries equal mass $m$. The center of mass of the system is at the average position of the three masses, which is exactly $G$.

If instead the entire (uniform) triangular region carries mass, the center of mass is still at the same $G$ — by symmetry and the fact that the medians divide the triangle into equal-area pieces (each of area = total/6).

md: ### Where the centroid sits
**Always inside the triangle** — for every triangle (acute, right, obtuse, scalene, isosceles, equilateral). Unlike the circumcenter and orthocenter, the centroid never escapes the triangle.
**Equilateral triangle:** $G$ coincides with the circumcenter, incenter, and orthocenter.
**Isosceles triangle:** $G$ lies on the axis of symmetry.
